summary: In Islam, Maryam (Mary) is the mother of the prophet Isa and among the most highly honored figures in the Qur'an; she is a virgin who conceived Isa by God's command, is called chosen and purified above the women of the worlds (Q3:42), and is the only woman named in the Qur'an, which devotes Surah Maryam (Q19) to her.
description: Maryam bint 'Imran holds a place of exceptional honor in the Qur'an, which names her more than any other woman and gives her a surah of her own, Surah Maryam (Q19). The Qur'an narrates her dedication to God's service before birth, her upbringing under the care of Zakariyya, and the annunciation in which an angel announces that God will grant her a pure son though no man has touched her; when she asks how this can be, the answer is that God creates what He wills and needs only to say 'Be' (Q3:45-47, Q19:16-21). Islam thus affirms the virginal conception of Isa, presenting it as a sign (aya) of God's power rather than as evidence of any divine sonship. Q3:42 records that the angels told her God had chosen her, purified her, and chosen her above the women of the worlds, and Q66:12 names her among the models of faith as one who guarded her chastity and believed in the words of her Lord. Classical and later Muslim scholarship debated her exact rank, including whether she should be counted a prophetess (nabiyya) or a supremely righteous saint (siddiqa); the more common view treats prophethood as reserved to men while affirming her as the highest of women. Throughout, the Qur'anic Maryam is honored as a devout servant of God and a sign, never as an object of worship, consistent with the boundary that tawhid draws around God alone.
editorial_notes: The entry describes Maryam on Islam's own terms first (neutrality #6): a supremely honored human being, a virgin who conceived Isa by God's command, chosen and purified above women, and a sign of God's power, never an object of worship. The debated question of her rank (prophetess versus supremely righteous woman) is reported as an intra-Islamic scholarly discussion without adjudication (#1, #2). Sourcing triangulates three lenses per #8-9: outside/academic scholarship on the Qur'anic Jesus and his mother (Robinson, whose study of the Qur'anic Jesus treats the annunciation and virginal conception), the tradition's own primary scripture (Surah Maryam Q19, Surah Al Imran Q3:42-47, and Q66:12), and a contemporary Muslim scholar (Aliah Schleifer, whose monograph on Mary in Islam is by a Muslim academic and grew out of her doctoral work, filling the modern Muslim scholarly lens). No encyclopedias or tertiary sources. Confidence is medium except for the primary Qur'anic text. Relations are deliberately minimal: related_to isa (created in this batch) is the core link; related_to nubuwwa is included because her exact standing relative to prophethood is a live question in the tradition, which is the apt frame the prompt allows. No compares_with link is asserted here; a Mary comparison across traditions is a candidate for a future batch and is not invented now. VERIFIED (2026-07-19): the load-bearing Qur'anic verses (Q 3:42-47, 19:16-34, 66:12) were read directly at quran.com and confirmed (Q 3:42 'chosen above the women of the worlds' verbatim), with a second accuracy check; the Qur'an source carries honest high confidence and the entry is promoted to verified. The secondary works remain medium (not read line by line).

relations.note: Maryam is the mother of the prophet Isa, who is always named ibn Maryam (son of Mary); the Qur'an ties her virginal conception directly to his birth as a sign of God.

Maryam's standing relative to prophethood (nubuwwa) is debated in the tradition: some scholars counted her a prophetess (nabiyya), while the more common view reserves prophethood to men and ranks her the highest of women. The link marks that open question, not a settled classification.

historical_period.note: The Islamic account of Maryam is given in the Qur'an (revealed 610-632 CE), above all in Surah Maryam (Q19) and Surah Al Imran (Q3), and elaborated in the classical tafsir and qisas al-anbiya literature.

sources.citation: Robinson, Neal. Christ in Islam and Christianity: The Representation of Jesus in the Qur'an and the Classical Muslim Commentaries. Albany: State University of New York Press, 1991. Academic study whose treatment of the virginal conception and annunciation covers the Qur'anic presentation of Maryam.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: The Qur'an, Surah Maryam (19:16-34), Surah Al Imran (3:42-47), and Surah al-Tahrim (66:12). Primary scripture: the annunciation and virginal conception, the statement that God chose and purified Maryam above the women of the worlds, and her naming among the models of faith.
sources.license: Primary religious text; classical Arabic in the public domain.

sources.citation: Schleifer, Aliah. Mary the Blessed Virgin of Islam. Louisville, KY: Fons Vitae, 1997 (based on the author's doctoral dissertation, University of Exeter). A monograph by a Muslim academic surveying the Qur'anic and classical exegetical portrayal of Maryam, including the debate over whether she should be regarded as having received revelation. Serves as the contemporary Muslim scholarly lens.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.
